name: Prompt-requested Labels on Generated Short Answers Are Noisy Without Manual Relabeling
statement: Bexte and Zesch show that generated short answers often fail to conform to the requested target labels; models trained on as-generated labels underperform models trained on original SRA data, while manually relabeled generated data can support competitive content-scoring models.
population_or_context: Short-answer supervised content scoring on the Student Response Analysis dataset.
quantitative_note: The paper reports macro-averaged F1 comparisons; its conclusion states that up to 75% of generated answers requested for a specific label did not conform to that label.
caveats: The finding is bounded to one SRA setup, five-way labels, DeepSeek-v2, and the tested zero-shot generation prompt.
Manual relabeling improves utility but adds human labor, so the result is not evidence of free synthetic scoring labels.
Generated answer language differed from student-authored answer language.
evaluation_method: Label-conformance analysis, macro-averaged F1 on original student answers, and comparison between as-generated and manually cleaned labels.
reported_metrics: Macro-averaged F1 and label-conformance findings, including the reported up-to-75% nonconformance for requested labels.
limitations: Does not generalize across all LLMs or score scales.
The study evaluates content-scoring training utility, not human-AI agreement on final operational scoring.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase_note: The finding paraphrases the generated-label setup, macro-F1 comparison, and conclusion about label nonconformance and manual relabeling.
